flashcards.front: What is the primary definition of an Operating System?
flashcards.back: An Operating System is system software that acts as an intermediary between computer hardware and user applications, managing hardware resources while providing a consistent interface for software execution.

flashcards.front: What are the two fundamental views of an Operating System?
flashcards.back: The two fundamental views are: (1) OS as a RESOURCE MANAGER - manages CPU, memory, I/O devices, and (2) OS as an EXTENDED MACHINE - provides user-friendly interface hiding hardware complexity.

flashcards.front: What is the main function of the OS as a resource manager?
flashcards.back: The OS coordinates and manages all system resources including processor time, main memory, disk storage, and I/O devices, ensuring efficient and fair allocation among competing programs.

flashcards.front: List the five major functions of an Operating System.
flashcards.back: The five major functions are: (1) PROCESS MANAGEMENT - creating, scheduling, and terminating processes, (2) MEMORY MANAGEMENT - allocating and deallocating memory, (3) FILE MANAGEMENT - organizing and managing files, (4) DEVICE MANAGEMENT - handling I/O devices, (5) SECURITY AND PROTECTION - protecting system resources.

A System Call is the programming interface through which user programs request services from the OS kernel. Examples include fork(), exec(), read(), write(), and open() for process, file, and device operations.

flashcards.front: What is the Kernel of an Operating System?
flashcards.back: The Kernel is the core component of the OS that runs in privileged mode, managing critical tasks like process scheduling, memory management, and hardware interactions. It remains in memory at all times.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between CLI and GUI?
flashcards.back: CLI (Command Line Interface) requires users to type text commands, while GUI (Graphical User Interface) provides visual elements like windows, icons, and menus for user interaction.
Multiprogramming is a technique where multiple programs are loaded in memory and the CPU switches between them, maximizing CPU utilization by keeping it busy when one program waits for I/O.

Time-Sharing is an extension of multiprogramming where the CPU rapidly switches between multiple users, giving each user the illusion of dedicated processor time, typically used in interactive systems.

flashcards.front: What are the main types of Operating Systems?
flashcards.back: The main types are: BATCH OS (processes grouped and run sequentially), MULTIPROGRAMMING OS (multiple jobs in memory), TIME-SHARING OS (interactive multi-user), REAL-TIME OS (immediate response), DISTRIBUTED OS (multiple networked computers), and NETWORK OS (manages network resources).

flashcards.front: What is a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)?
flashcards.back: An RTOS is designed to process data and respond to inputs within strict time constraints (deadlines). It is used in embedded systems like medical devices, industrial controls, and aerospace systems.

flashcards.front: What is the boot process of a computer?
flashcards.back: The boot process involves: (1) Power-on self-test (POST), (2) Loading BIOS/UEFI firmware, (3) Reading boot loader from MBR/EFI, (4) Loading OS kernel into memory, (5) Initializing system services, (6) Starting user interface.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between system software and application software?
flashcards.back: System software manages and controls computer hardware and provides a platform for running application software (examples: OS, compilers). Application software performs specific user tasks (examples: word processors, web browsers).

OS Architecture defines the structure and design of the operating system. Common architectures include MONOLITHIC (all services in kernel), MICROKERNEL (minimal kernel with user-space services), LAYERED (hierarchical layers), and HYBRID (combination of approaches).

flashcards.front: What is Virtualization in the context of OS?
flashcards.back: Virtualization allows multiple operating systems to run on a single physical machine by creating virtual instances of hardware. The software layer (hypervisor) manages resource allocation between virtual machines.
